stories.headline: Aurora Officer Stabbed in Head with Butcher Knife Near Cherry Creek State Park; Suspect Dead
stories.summary:
An Aurora Police Department K-9 officer was stabbed multiple times in the head with a butcher knife Thursday afternoon during a confrontation near Cherry Creek State Park, and the suspect was fatally shot by responding officers. The officer, a 20-plus-year veteran who has served on the K-9 Unit since 2012, was in surgery as of 6 p.m. Thursday but is expected to survive, according to APD Chief Todd Chamberlain.

The incident began around 3:15 p.m. when Aurora Mental Health placed a call reporting a 23-year-old man who was threatening to kill himself and others at a residence in the 14000 block of East Stanford Circle. Officers responded alongside the department's Critical Incident Response Team, which includes medical clinicians who are also sworn officers, and spent roughly 16 minutes attempting phone contact with the suspect before he emerged from the residence.

The suspect, who had a history of suicidal ideations and prior mental health holds, burst out the front door carrying a large butcher knife and charged at the K-9 officer at full speed, stabbing him repeatedly in the head with such force that a portion of the blade broke off. Officers then shot and killed the suspect.

Chief Chamberlain described the attack as 'without question, a tragedy' and emphasized that officers had deployed a range of less-lethal tools and de-escalation measures before the encounter turned violent. The incident is under review by Aurora's independent investigation process.

stories.headline: Challenger Melat Kiros Gains Momentum Against Rep. Diana DeGette in Denver Democratic Primary
stories.summary:
A 28-year-old barista, lawyer, and Ph.D. student named Melat Kiros is mounting a serious challenge to longtime U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette in the Democratic primary for Colorado's 1st Congressional District. Kiros, a Democratic socialist, defeated DeGette at the Denver County assembly last month with 63% of the vote to DeGette's 32% — the first time DeGette had lost a county assembly vote since first winning her congressional seat in 1996.

Despite the assembly upset, DeGette narrowly secured her place on the June 30 primary ballot by winning 33% support at the 1st Congressional District party assembly, just above the 30% threshold required. A third candidate, University of Colorado Regent Wanda James, has filed a petition to also make the ballot, with her signatures still under review by the state.

Kiros and her supporters argue the assembly result reflects broader Democratic voter frustration, comparing her rise to that of New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani. State Rep. Javier Mabrey, who endorsed Kiros, said the race taps into an energy among voters who want more transformative change than the current congressional majority offers.

Political observers caution that the assembly drew only a small fraction of the roughly 416,000 voters eligible to participate in the June primary — including 230,000 unaffiliated voters and 186,000 registered Democrats. DeGette's campaign called the assembly 'only a small first step with a small group of people.'

stories.headline: Jokic Records 34th Triple-Double as Nuggets Extend Win Streak to 10 Games with 136-119 Victory Over Grizzlies
stories.summary:
Nikola Jokic posted 14 points, 15 rebounds, and 10 assists for his 34th triple-double of the season Wednesday night, leading the Denver Nuggets to a 136-119 victory over the Memphis Grizzlies at Ball Arena. The win extended Denver's winning streak to 10 consecutive games — the team's first double-digit streak since a 15-game run from February to March 2013.

Jamal Murray led Denver in scoring with 26 points, while Jonas Valanciunas contributed 14 points in his 1,000th career NBA game. Jokic, who already clinched a triple-double statistical average for the second consecutive season, recorded his 198th career triple-double with an assist midway through the third quarter and did not play in the fourth.

The Nuggets improved to 52-28 on the season, strengthening their hold on the No. 3 seed in the Western Conference. Denver now leads the Los Angeles Lakers by 1.5 games in the standings, with Denver's final two regular-season games coming against No. 1 seed Oklahoma City on Friday and No. 2 San Antonio on Sunday.

Cedric Coward scored 27 points for Memphis, which has lost six straight and 18 of its last 20 games. The Grizzlies connected on 13 of 27 three-pointers in the first half to lead 72-68, but Denver outscored them 31-13 over the final 7:51 of the third quarter to take control.

stories.headline: Nuggets Playoff Seeding Scenarios: Denver Could Land Anywhere from No. 3 to No. 5 Seed in West
stories.summary:
With two regular-season games remaining, the Denver Nuggets sit at 52-28 and have clinched no worse than the No. 5 seed in the Western Conference but could finish as high as No. 3, depending on results involving the Houston Rockets and Los Angeles Lakers. The Nuggets hold a head-to-head advantage over Houston (3-1 in the season series) but trail Los Angeles in head-to-head matchups (2-1), meaning the Lakers would receive the higher seed in any two-way tie between those clubs.

Denver's remaining schedule features games against the top two teams in the conference — Oklahoma City on Friday and San Antonio on Sunday — though both opponents have already clinched their seeds and are expected to rest key players. The Lakers, meanwhile, are playing without Luka Doncic and Austin Reaves for the rest of the regular season, making it less likely they will sweep their final three games.

A No. 3 seed would pit Denver against No. 6 Minnesota in the first round — a rematch of recent playoff history. If Denver falls to the No. 4 or No. 5 seed, it would face either the Lakers or Rockets in the opening round, with home-court advantage at stake.

The most straightforward path to the No. 3 seed requires Denver winning at least one of its remaining two games or the Lakers losing at least one of theirs. A three-way tie among Denver, Houston, and Los Angeles would result in the Lakers as No. 3, Denver as No. 4, and Houston as No. 5.

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston Announces Expanded East Colfax Plan Pairing Workforce Housing with New Police Station
stories.summary:
Mayor Mike Johnston announced an expanded development plan for East Colfax Avenue that will pair a new Denver Police Department District 6 headquarters with a mixed-use complex featuring ground-floor retail and workforce housing above. The city intends to demolish the vacant Pharmacy Building adjacent to the existing police station and offer the site to developers at little or no cost, with the winning bidder required to build the housing and commercial project.

The original plan called only for renovating the existing station and selling the surrounding land, but Johnston revised the proposal after pushback from community members and businesses who wanted housing included. The new police facility will be built at East Colfax Avenue and Washington Street using funds approved by voters in the 2017 Elevate Denver Bond. The city plans to issue a development bid this summer, begin demolition this year, and break ground on the new station this fall.

Councilman Chris Hinds praised the dual-site approach as a strategic investment in public safety and housing simultaneously. Denver needs to create nearly 44,000 affordable housing units over the next decade, according to the city's Department of Housing Stability, making the project one piece of a much larger challenge.

The announcement comes alongside other Johnston administration housing initiatives including blanket zoning changes and fast-track permitting. The Colfax Bus Rapid Transit line is in its final stages of construction, which officials say will increase the corridor's appeal to residents and businesses.

stories.headline: Denver Property Management Company Pays Nearly $70,000 to Settle State Claims of Illegal Renters Insurance Charges
stories.summary:
Baron Properties, a Denver-based property management company, will pay the state of Colorado nearly $70,000 to resolve allegations that it illegally charged tenants for renters insurance and used unlawful language in rental applications, the Colorado Attorney General's Office announced Tuesday. The settlement includes $7,300 in restitution to 368 tenants and $67,635 paid to the state.

State investigators found that Baron charged some tenants for renters insurance even after those tenants had already provided proof of their own coverage. The company operates two properties: the Belmont Buckingham at 1050 N. Sherman St. in Denver and Belcaro, a senior living community in Colorado Springs. Officials also found that the company's rental applications treated pending or unresolved criminal cases as convictions, in violation of the Rental Application Fairness Act.

Attorney General Phil Weiser said the conduct was unacceptable given the financial pressure many Colorado renters already face. Baron Properties President Matthew Riggs disputed the characterization, saying the company strongly objects to any notion of intentional deception and that most overcharges were minor personnel and software errors that have since been corrected.

Riggs said Baron has worked with the attorney general's office for several years and began reimbursing overcharged tenants after first learning of the issue. The company agreed to modify its rental application language but maintained it had never denied an application based solely on an arrest record or pending charge.

stories.headline: Metro Denver Drought Restrictions Spread: Aurora, Arvada, Golden, Brighton and More Enact Outdoor Watering Limits
stories.summary:
A growing number of metro Denver communities are implementing drought restrictions on outdoor water use in response to record-low snowpack in the Colorado mountains. Aurora declared a Stage 1 water shortage this week with immediate restrictions on outdoor watering, and Arvada followed with its own Stage 1 declaration. Golden announced it will enact Stage 1 restrictions beginning May 1, prohibiting daytime sprinkler use and banning vehicle washing.

Brighton has also joined the list of Front Range cities rolling out drought watering restrictions, while Boulder has urged residents to reduce consumption as the city enters a drought watch status. Broomfield has declared a drought watch as well. Denver Water previously enacted Stage 1 drought pricing for its 1.5 million customers for the first time since 2013.

The cascade of restrictions reflects the severity of this year's water supply situation across the Front Range. Officials in multiple cities have cited the risk of reservoir shortfalls if the dry conditions persist into summer.

Residents under Stage 1 restrictions are generally prohibited from watering lawns during peak daylight hours and may face fines for runoff or overwatering. Local governments have encouraged residents to check their specific provider's rules, as restrictions vary by utility and municipality.

stories.headline: Lawsuit Alleges JBS Greeley Meatpacking Plant Has Operated Without Valid Federal Air Pollution Permit Since 2021
stories.summary:
The Center for Biological Diversity filed a lawsuit Tuesday in Weld County District Court alleging that the JBS-owned Swift Beef Co. meatpacking plant in Greeley has been operating without a valid Title V federal air pollution permit since January 2021. The suit names the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment and its Air Pollution Control Division as defendants, accusing state regulators of failing to finalize a permit renewal within the 18-month window required under the Clean Air Act.

The plant's permit renewal application was originally due January 27, 2021, but the company did not file it until April 20, 2022 — more than a year late. Under federal law, the state should have finalized the permit by October 2023, making it at least two and a half years overdue. JBS is not named as a defendant in the suit.

Jeremy Nichols, a senior advocate with the Center for Biological Diversity, said the lawsuit aims to compel state regulators to act and send a message that companies cannot operate above the law. The Air Pollution Control Division declined to comment on the pending litigation.

In a statement, JBS spokeswoman Nikki Richardson acknowledged the late application but said the company has worked in good faith with state regulators and believes it is meeting all current expectations. The center argues that operating under an outdated permit potentially allows for more pollution than a renewed permit with updated, stricter controls would authorize.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs Mayor Mobolade Hires Former Election Rival Wayne Williams as Chief of Staff
stories.summary:
Colorado Springs Mayor Yemi Mobolade has hired Wayne Williams, the Republican he defeated by 15 points in the 2023 mayoral election, to serve as his chief of staff. Williams, who previously served as Colorado Secretary of State and held elected office for nearly two decades, will help run the city for Mobolade, Colorado Springs' first non-Republican mayor in recent history.

Mobolade, who is unaffiliated with any party, said he wanted the best available talent and aimed to demonstrate a form of leadership that prioritizes residents' quality of life over partisan considerations. Williams said he hopes the partnership serves as a model for breaking through political gridlock nationally, noting that communities function best when people with differing views choose to work together.

The two men sat down with 9NEWS anchor Kyle Clark for an exclusive interview prior to Thursday's announcement. Both said they agreed on the most pressing issues facing Colorado Springs during their 2023 contest but differed on approaches, and that mutual respect developed from how Williams handled his concession — including a visit to Mobolade's victory party on election night.

When asked whether the hire was intended to neutralize Williams as a potential 2027 mayoral challenger, Mobolade said that was not his motivation, pointing instead to Williams' extensive governing experience. Williams remains a registered Republican active in state party politics.

stories.headline: Colorado Lawmakers Advance Several Family Court Reform Bills Covering Pets, Child Standing, and Domestic Violence
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators from both parties are pursuing a series of bills to reshape how the state's family courts handle divorce, custody, and child welfare proceedings, though several proposals have already stalled. House Bill 1131, sponsored by Rep. Alex Valdez, would allow courts to make custody decisions for pets in divorce cases and authorize emergency protection orders for animals; it has passed its first committee and awaits an Appropriations hearing, with a two-year fiscal note exceeding $1.5 million.

House Bill 1227 would give children legal standing in dependency and neglect proceedings and the right to contest county dismissals before a ruling. The bipartisan bill, sponsored by Denver Rep. Lindsay Gilchrist, Colorado Springs Rep. Mary Bradfield, and Thornton Sen. Kyle Mullica, has also cleared its first committee with an estimated two-year cost of about $460,000.

House Bill 1309 would require courts to determine whether domestic violence occurred when weighing a child's best interests in parenting time decisions, and would mandate that accused parents complete a one-year abuser intervention program. Two Republican-backed bills that would have established a presumption of equal parenting time and created remedies for parents falsely accused of abuse both failed to advance through their first committees.

The broader package reflects ongoing debate at the Capitol over how best to protect children and fairly adjudicate family disputes. Several of the advancing bills carry multi-million dollar fiscal notes that will be scrutinized in the Appropriations Committee.

stories.headline: Colorado Board of Education Unanimously Rejects Application for New Online Technical High School
stories.summary:
The Colorado Board of Education voted unanimously Thursday to deny the application for the Colorado Virtual Tech Academy, a proposed online school for grades 6-12 that would have specialized in technical and postsecondary education. The board cited Colorado's already crowded virtual school landscape — the state has 44 online schools — and persistently high dropout rates among existing programs as key reasons for the denial.

The application was submitted by Education ReEnvisioned Board of Cooperative Educational Services, an El Paso County organization that already authorizes several online schools. The proposed curriculum would have been provided by Stellar Virtual, a national virtual school company with no existing presence in Colorado, and Penn Foster for postsecondary programming.

Board members raised concerns that Stellar Virtual's proposed goals were not written to meet Colorado's academic standards and would have required significant adjustments. They also flagged a pattern of online schools in the state where a high percentage of students opt out of accountability standards, creating what one board member called 'an accountability blackout.'

Proponents argued the school would fill a gap in Colorado's virtual offerings by focusing on vocational and career-technical pathways rather than college preparation. Despite those arguments, the board concluded the state does not need additional virtual schools given existing capacity and performance concerns.

stories.headline: Weekend Events Guide: Boulder Film Festival, Denver Silent Film Festival, Arvada Kite Festival and More
stories.summary:
Metro Denver and surrounding communities are hosting a full slate of events April 10-12, headlined by the 22nd annual Boulder International Film Festival, which runs through Sunday and features films, filmmakers, and related programming across the city. In Denver, the Silent Film Festival at the Sie FilmCenter presents restored classics on the big screen with live musical accompaniment through Sunday.

The Arvada Kite Festival celebrates its 21st year on Sunday from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at the Stenger Sports Complex, while the Tartan Day Festival at the Boulder County Fairgrounds in Longmont runs both Saturday and Sunday starting at 9 a.m., celebrating Scottish and Celtic heritage. The Northern Colorado Children's Festival takes place Saturday at Island Grove Regional Park in Greeley from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m.

The Bud Light Rocks the Boat free concert series wraps up Sunday in Steamboat Springs with back-to-back performances beginning at 2:30 p.m. at Steamboat Square. The inaugural Denver Jazz Fest, previously highlighted in this digest, continues through Sunday across 15 venues in Denver and Boulder.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I believe that sex is one of the most beautiful, natural, wholesome things that money can buy.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Jazz Fest – Free Shows at Embassy Suites
weekendEvents.description: The second annual Denver Jazz Fest runs through Sunday with free nightly performances in the lobby of the downtown Embassy Suites. Friday features Laurie Dameron & Jeff Fournier, Saturday features Clint Dadian & Scott Grove, and Sunday features Tom Amend & Nate Miller.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Jazz Fest – Free Concert at Union Station
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Jazz Festival presents a free performance in the Great Hall of Union Station featuring Daryl Gott on alto sax, Seth Lewis on bass, and Tom Amend on piano.
weekendEvents.location: Great Hall, Union Station, 1701 Wynkoop Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Explicitly Funny Comedy
weekendEvents.description: A stand-up comedy showcase produced by Joe Huisman featuring unfiltered, explicit humor including raunchy storytelling, dark punchlines, and brutally honest material. Tickets start at $20.

weekendEvents.title: Mini Masters Bar Crawl: 6-Hole Tennyson Street Putt Putt
weekendEvents.description: A Masters-themed bar crawl along Tennyson Street where participants work through six locations, each featuring a unique mini golf hole and optional specialty drink. Tickets are $20 and proceeds benefit the Colorado State Beekeepers Association.
weekendEvents.location: Starting at Alley Brews Brewery & Coffee Shop, 4342 Tennyson Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: How To: Researching Your Home
weekendEvents.description: A class at History Colorado Center led by Liv Wisnewski, Temple Buell assistant curator of architecture, covering how to research your property's history using public records, historical maps, and online databases. Tickets are $5.
weekendEvents.location: History Colorado Center, 1200 North Broadway, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Whose Scene Is It Anyway?
weekendEvents.description: A local improv game show at What If Theatre in Lakewood in which audience contestants cast scenes from popular movies for comedic actors to improvise. April's show features Heather Curran and the cast of ABC. Tickets are $15 general admission, $12 for kids, seniors, and military.
weekendEvents.location: What If Theatre, 9060 West 6th Avenue, Lakewood

weekendEvents.title: 5280 Brunch Event
weekendEvents.description: 5280 Magazine's annual brunch event offers bites from editor-selected restaurants, unlimited signature drinks including a build-your-own Bloody Mary station, and music from DJ Mr. Groove. Tickets are $62–$67.

weekendEvents.title: Arvada Kite Festival
weekendEvents.description: A free community festival featuring kites of all shapes in the sky, more than 100 vendor booths, arts and crafts, food trucks, and a Nerf Gun Zone. Attendance is free.
weekendEvents.location: Arvada (see event website for specific location)

weekendEvents.title: Volo Women's Walk at Sloan's Lake
weekendEvents.description: Community rec league Volo hosts its first Women's Walk, beginning with a 60-minute outdoor yoga session followed by a group walk around Sloan's Lake. An after-party at Lot 46 Music Bar follows with $6 drink specials. Yoga is $8 for non-members, $3 for Volo Pass members.
weekendEvents.location: Sloan's Lake Park, 1700 North Sheridan Boulevard, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Boulder International Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: The 22nd annual Boulder International Film Festival runs through Sunday with screenings including a documentary on poet Mary Oliver, a Jay Duplass film featuring Denver comedian Adam Cayton-Holland, and The Trial of Alec Baldwin with Baldwin in attendance for a live interview. Ticket packs start at $35.

weekendEvents.title: Adult Book Fair at New Terrain Brewing
weekendEvents.description: New Terrain Brewing hosts a book fair for adults featuring local authors, Boulder's Sugar & Spice Books, the Wandering Book Co mobile library, and a mix of indie booksellers, artists, and makers.

topStory.headline: Strait of Hormuz Remains Largely Closed as U.S.-Iran Ceasefire Holds Uneasily; Trump Warns Against Toll Charges
topStory.summary:
A fragile ceasefire between the United States and Iran appeared to be holding Friday, but the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed to normal commercial traffic, with only a handful of vessels transiting the waterway in the first 24 hours after the truce was announced. Ship-tracking data showed between five and nine bulk carriers had passed through the strait, compared with a prewar average of more than 100 vessels per day. Oil prices rose above $100 a barrel as initial optimism over the ceasefire gave way to uncertainty about the route's future status.

Iran has asserted it retains the right to require permission for ships to transit the strait and has suggested it may impose fees for passage, a position that directly contradicts U.S. demands for a full reopening as a condition of the truce. The Iranian navy released a navigation map indicating possible mining operations in portions of the waterway and directing vessels along new lanes closer to Iran's mainland. President Trump publicly cautioned Iran against charging tolls for passage, while Sultan Al Jaber, chief executive of Abu Dhabi's national oil company and a UAE government minister, stated bluntly that the strait was 'not open' and that access was 'being restricted, conditioned and controlled.'

Ceasefire violations were disputed on multiple fronts. Kuwait accused Iran and its proxies of launching drone attacks against it on Thursday despite the truce, and Saudi Arabia said its East-West oil pipeline — a key route that bypasses the strait — was damaged in recent attacks. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps denied responsibility for any strikes since the ceasefire began, asserting that reports of attacks on Persian Gulf states were 'undoubtedly the work of the Zionist enemy or the United States.' The IRGC stated that any Iranian military action would be officially announced.

Elsewhere, the U.S. warned Iraq that it must dismantle Iran-aligned militia groups operating on its soil or risk straining bilateral ties, following drone attacks near the Baghdad Diplomatic Support Center and Baghdad International Airport. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau conveyed the warning to Iraq's ambassador, citing hundreds of attacks on U.S. personnel, diplomatic facilities, and commercial interests in recent weeks. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy separately confirmed for the first time that Ukrainian military personnel had participated in shooting down Iranian-designed Shahed drones in multiple Middle Eastern countries during the conflict, describing the operations as active combat support rather than training.

The broader situation remains volatile as U.S. and Iranian officials prepare for planned negotiations. Industry groups, including BIMCO, said shipowners were waiting for technical guidance from both governments before resuming normal transit. The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and natural gas, making its status a central economic and geopolitical concern for energy markets and Gulf states dependent on export revenues.
